title: Rustic Three-Drawer Bathroom Vanity
summary: A floor-standing vanity with edge-glued 1x6 side panels, a pocket-holed face frame, and three real drawers on euro slides — sized to build your own top around your own sink.
description:
The sides here are three 1x6 boards glued edge to edge on a flat surface rather than a plywood panel, giving the vanity a genuine board-and-batten look once the 1x2 trim caps the top. Build the front face frame as its own flat assembly, then nail and glue it to the front, checking for square by comparing diagonal measurements before moving on.

**The interior back pieces aren't just a finished surface — they're what the drawer slides actually attach to**, while still hiding the plywood-free construction so no raw edges show from behind. Build full-size drawers with pocket holes where there's no plumbing to work around; where a drawer has to clear plumbing, keep the same basic cuts but box out the corners and set the back where it needs to pass the pipe.

**The top is intentionally open-ended — build it to suit your own sink**, using 1x boards pocket-holed together with a cutout sized to your actual sink rather than a fixed opening. Drawer slides mount to the front 1x2 cleat and a rear end socket; follow your hardware's own instructions for spacing.

tools.note: For cutting the sink opening in the top.

materials.name: Euro style drawer slides, 16 in, pair
Primer and paint, or stain and poly

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: From the 1x6s cut six side panel boards and two back panel boards at 31 3/4", four large drawer sides at 16", and four large drawer front/back at 23 3/4" — that fills all five 8 ft boards with about half an inch of slack on the tightest one, so measure twice before every cut. From the 1x4s cut two front legs at 31 3/4", two top drawer sides at 16", and two top drawer front/back at 23 3/4". From the 1x3s cut two back supports at 32" and two face frame top/bottom rails at 26 1/2"; from the 1x2s cut four side trim at 18", two face frame middle rails at 26 1/2", two drawer slide supports at 31 3/4", and two front trim at 35" left long to trim to fit later. Cut three drawer bottoms 16" x 25 1/4" from the 1/4" plywood. The cut list carries no parts for the top and the 1x4 allowance will not cover one either — that is deliberate, since the top is built around your own sink, so buy that material separately once you have chosen it.

steps.title: Build the side panels
steps.body: Glue three 31 3/4" 1x6 boards edge to edge on a flat surface for each side panel — three 5 1/2" boards give a side 16 1/2" deep and 31 3/4" tall. Clamp across the joints and check the panel is flat before the glue sets; a cupped side panel will never let the face frame sit square. Attach the 18" 1x2 trim on top, which is that 16 1/2" depth plus the 3/4" of overhang at each end. Leave a 2 1/2" space under the bottom trim.

steps.title: Attach the back supports
steps.body: Drill two pocket holes at each end of the two 32" back support boards and screw them BETWEEN the two side panels, with the pocket holes exposed on the back where nobody sees them. This is what sets the cabinet's width: 32" plus the two 3/4" sides is 33 1/2", which is exactly the face frame you build next — 26 1/2" rails plus two 3 1/2" front legs. Set one support at the top and one at the bottom, flush with the back edges of the sides. Compare the diagonals now, because everything downstream is squared to this box.

steps.title: Build and attach the face frame
steps.body: Build the frame flat: the two 31 3/4" 1x4 front legs run the full height, and the 26 1/2" rails fit between them — two 1x3 at the top and bottom, two 1x2 in the middle — giving a 33 1/2" wide frame that matches the carcass exactly. The four rails between the legs take about 8" of height, so the three openings total roughly 23 3/4" — but the three drawer boxes are only 3 1/2" + 5 1/2" + 5 1/2" = 14 1/2" tall, and the cut list has no separate drawer-front parts. Space the middle rails to fit the boxes you build and leave the leftover opening as open space (or add face stock yourself); do not invent faces the plan does not sell. Join the frame with pocket holes and 1 1/4" pocket hole screws, then nail and glue it to the front of the cabinet. Check for square by comparing diagonal measurements before the glue sets.

steps.title: Attach the back panels
steps.body: Nail and glue the back panel boards to the sides and top/bottom supports, giving the drawer slides something solid to attach to.

steps.title: Finish the front trim
steps.body: Measure and cut the front trim to fit, then nail and glue in place.

steps.title: Build the drawers
steps.body: Build all three boxes 16" deep: the 16" sides run the FULL depth and the fronts and backs fit BETWEEN them, so at 23 3/4" each box comes out 25 1/4" wide overall — exactly the 16" x 25 1/4" plywood bottoms, which is your check that you have it the right way round. Two boxes are 5 1/2" tall out of 1x6 and the top one is 3 1/2" tall out of 1x4. Join the corners with 3/4" pocket holes and 1 1/4" pocket hole screws, then nail and glue a plywood bottom onto each. Where a drawer has to clear plumbing, keep these same cuts but box out the corner and set the back forward of the pipe rather than resizing the box.

steps.title: Install the drawer slides
steps.body: A 25 1/4" box in the 26 1/2" face frame opening leaves about 5/8" per side, which is what the 16" euro slides are meant to take up — confirm that against your own hardware before you drill anything, because brands differ. Mount the front of each slide to the 1x2 drawer slide support and the back into a rear end socket screwed to the back panel boards, which is why those boards went on before this step. Follow the slide's own template for height and setback rather than a number from this plan, and set the two sides of each pair at exactly the same height or the drawer will bind.
materials: Euro style drawer slides, 16 in, pair

steps.title: Build the top
steps.body: Build the top from 1x boards pocket-holed together, leaving an opening sized to your own sink.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Sand all surfaces, apply primer and paint or stain and poly, and add the bin pulls.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly
